did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning architectures, specifically fine-tuned DCNNs (VGG16, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, DenseNet 201) and an ensemble model, to classify skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the fine-tuning of deep learning architectures (VGG16, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, DenseNet 201) on the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin lesions. It includes a methodology section, experimental results with specific accuracy metrics (e.g., 88.46% validation accuracy for the ensemble model), and a comparison with existing works, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Skin Lesion Classification using Deep Learning Architectures
journal: 2024 Second International Conference on Network, Multimedia and Information Technology (NMITCON)
brief_summary: This study evaluates deep convolutional neural networks for skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers fine-tuned architectures including VGG16, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, and DenseNet 201, comparing the efficacy of fine-tuning only the top layers versus the entire model. Experimental results demonstrated that fine-tuning all layers yielded superior performance and faster convergence than fine-tuning just the top layers. Among individual models, DenseNet 201 achieved the highest accuracy with 85.9% on validation and 83.4% on testing when only top layers were fine-tuned. The study further developed an ensemble model combining Inception V3 and DenseNet 201, which achieved the best overall results with 88.46% validation accuracy and 88.24% test accuracy. These findings highlight the effectiveness of ensemble learning and full model fine-tuning for improving early skin cancer detection.

paper_terms: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial cancer/Bowen's illness
lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 7 diagnostic categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (7 classes: Actinic keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Vascular lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi, Benign Keratosis-like lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The models (VGG16, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, DenseNet 201, and an Ensemble of Inception V3 and DenseNet 201) predict discrete diagnostic labels for skin lesions. Performance is measured by accuracy (e.g., 88.24% test accuracy for the ensemble model).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising 10,015 high-quality dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions from various populations and acquisition modalities. The dataset includes metadata with ground truth determined via histopathological examination (histo), expert consensus (consensus), in-vivo confocal microscopy (confocal), or follow-up examination (follow-up).
classes_used: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial cancer/Bowen's illness (akiec)

use.training: Used for training the Baseline CNN, VGG16, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, and DenseNet 201 models, with data augmentation applied.
use.validation: Used for validation to monitor performance during fine-tuning and to report validation accuracy metrics.
use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate final model performance and report test accuracy metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

experiment_fine_tuning_top_layers.metric: Validation Accuracy / Test Accuracy / Test Loss
experiment_fine_tuning_top_layers.notes: DenseNet201 achieved the highest validation and test accuracy among individual models when only top layers were fine-tuned. Inception ResNetV2 showed slightly better validation and test accuracy than VGG16 and InceptionV3.

experiment_fine_tuning_all_layers.metric: Validation Accuracy / Test Accuracy / Test Loss
experiment_fine_tuning_all_layers.notes: Fine-tuning all layers yielded superior results in less time (20 epochs vs 30 epochs) compared to fine-tuning only top layers. The Ensemble model combining Inception V3 and DenseNet 201 demonstrated the best performance with the lowest test loss and highest test accuracy.

state_of_the_art_comparison.notes: The proposed ensemble model outperformed existing works listed in the comparison.

architecture_used.main_model: Baseline CNN, VGG16, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, DenseNet 201, and an Ensemble of Inception V3 and DenseNet 201
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models were used for transfer learning (specifically mentioned for VGG16, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, and DenseNet 201). The Baseline CNN was constructed from scratch.

Baseline CNN.description: Compact CNN with layers having 16, 32, and 64 kernels respectively, each with 3x3 size and appropriate padding. Followed by max-pooling layers with 2x2 windows.

VGG16.description: Refined variant (VGG16 D) with four additional Convolutional layers in the third, fourth, and fifth convolutional blocks. Top fully connected layers removed and replaced with new layers for 7-class classification.

Inception V3.description: Integrates 11 inception blocks with smaller and larger convolutions and 1x1 convolution for dimensionality reduction.

Inception ResNet V2.description: Merges Inception modules with Residual connections.

DenseNet 201.description: Comprises four dense blocks with densely connected layers where every layer passes feature maps to all subsequent layers. Uses transition layers with convolution and pooling operations.

Ensemble.description: Combines fine-tuned Inception V3 and DenseNet 201 architectures.

learning_rate.baseline: 0.01 (with decay strategy: halving when validation accuracy plateaus for 3 epochs)

training_details.data_augmentation: Applied during training for all models
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7 processor, 16 GB RAM, 512 GB SSD, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2070

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layers tailored to the specific classification task
classes_list: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial cancer/Bowen's illness (akiec)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation applied during training for all models to introduce variations and prevent encountering identical images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training for all models (Baseline CNN, VGG16, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, DenseNet 201) to introduce variations and prevent the model from encountering identical images in each epoch. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation, flip, zoom ranges) were not reported in the text.

conclusions: Fine-tuning the complete trained model (all layers) performs significantly better and requires fewer epochs (20 vs 30) and less computational time compared to fine-tuning only the top layers.

Among individual architectures, DenseNet 201 achieved the highest accuracy when only top layers were fine-tuned (85.9% validation, 83.4% test), outperforming VGG16, Inception V3, and Inception ResNet V2.
DenseNet 201 demonstrated high efficiency, consistently outperforming other models despite having fewer parameters than Inception ResNet V2.

The proposed ensemble model, combining Inception V3 and DenseNet 201, achieved the best overall performance with 88.46% validation accuracy and 88.24% test accuracy, along with the lowest test loss (0.4215).
The proposed ensemble model outperformed existing state-of-the-art works listed in the comparison, including Random Forest (87.32%) and other ensemble models (87%).
The study confirms the effectiveness of ensemble learning and full model fine-tuning for improving the accuracy of early skin cancer detection using the HAM10000 dataset.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and demographic biases (primarily European/Caucasian populations), limiting the generalizability of the results to other skin types or global populations.
The paper does not report external validation on independent datasets (e.g., ISIC 2019/2020), raising concerns about overfitting to the specific characteristics of the HAM10000 training/test split.

Data leakage risks are present as the HAM10000 dataset contains multiple images of the same lesion (identified by lesion_id); if the train/validation/test split was not performed at the patient/lesion level rather than the image level, performance metrics may be artificially inflated.
Specific data augmentation techniques (e.g., rotation, flip, zoom parameters) are not detailed, only stating that augmentation was applied, which hinders reproducibility.

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods is limited to a small subset of cited works (e.g., Random Forest, MobileNetV2+LSTM) and does not include recent, high-performing transformer-based or specialized dermatological models.

The paper reports accuracy as the primary metric but does not provide precision, recall, F1-score, or confusion matrices, which are critical for evaluating performance on imbalanced classes like Melanoma vs. Benign Nevi.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is reported for the performance differences between models, making it difficult to assess if the improvements are statistically robust.

The hardware specifications (Intel Core i7, RTX 2070) and software versions (TensorFlow 2.4.0) are outdated, potentially affecting reproducibility and computational efficiency claims in current contexts.
